text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr A. Rahman
Harbour View Health Centre

31 Quayside Road, South Shields, Tyne and Wear NE33 1TB
Dear Dr A. Rahman
Samantha Kerr, DOB: 14-02-1987, Hospital No: H937521 NHS No. 7432051986
Flat 4, 12 Marine Parade, North Shields, NE30 1LU 

Diagnoses/summary: Recurrent seizures of uncertain classification; prior investigations reviewed with no definitive epileptogenic focus identified to date

Neuroimaging/EEG: MRI brain (2019) reported as structurally unremarkable; routine EEG (2020) non-specific; ambulatory EEG (2022) without definitive lateralising features

Previous medication: Levetiracetam (behavioural adverse effects reported), sodium valproate (weight gain, tremor), carbamazepine (dizziness), lacosamide (limited efficacy), clobazam PRN (sedation), lamotrigine (current), perampanel (mood disturbance)

Not taken: topiramate, zonisamide, brivaracetam, eslicarbazepine, cenobamate, rufinamide

Comorbidities: Mild asthma; vitamin D insufficiency (on supplementation)

Social/background: Works offshore on a rotation (three weeks offshore, three weeks onshore). We have arranged telephone reviews to coincide with onshore periods to support adherence and safety planning. She lives with her partner and has an agreed workplace emergency protocol.

I reviewed Samantha Kerr today via a scheduled onshore clinic appointment. She attended alone. She reported that her current routine has been more predictable since her employer implemented a buddy system offshore and ensured ready access to her rescue medication. Over the last year, seizure control has been relatively stable; she suffers generalised tonic-clonic seizures twice monthly, with a maximum seizure-free span of five weeks. focal motor seizures occur 3 - 4 per day. She also has drop attacks clustering every month. No seizures have been noted since her previous review. She attributes this recent short-term stability to better sleep offshore and consistent dosing supported by her phone reminders.

Current medication:
- Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily
- Clobazam 10 mg at night PRN for clustering (used no more than 2 consecutive nights per month)
- Salbutamol inhaler PRN
- Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily

Adverse effects/other issues: She notes occasional word-finding difficulty and mild sleep fragmentation, both tolerable. No new mood concerns. No history of status epilepticus. Rescue protocol remains in place with offshore medics aware.

Workplace/risk: We confirmed restrictions remain in place for working at heights and lone working. She does not drive. Offshore colleagues have been provided with an updated seizure first-aid plan and an inventory of her medication supply is checked at each crew change. She carries a medical alert tag.

Plan:
- Continue lamotrigine at current dose while stability is maintained; no change today given absence of seizures since last review and acceptable tolerability.
- Maintain clobazam PRN protocol for monthly clusters with clear ceiling use; to inform us if clusters increase or sedation emerges.
- Repeat serum lamotrigine level and routine bloods (U&E, LFTs, FBC, vitamin D) at local phlebotomy when next onshore; results to be reviewed by telephone.
- Provide written medication plan to offshore medical team and confirm stock checks prior to each rotation.
- Seizure diary to continue, including offshore/onshore context, sleep patterns, and rescue use.
- Telephone reviews scheduled during onshore periods every 6–8 weeks, with option to bring forward if breakthrough events recur.
- Discussed, and patient is open to, consideration of alternative add-on therapy in future if needed; information provided for reading, no decision today.

I will arrange a routine follow-up in four months aligned with her onshore week, with interim telephone contact in 6–8 weeks. Please let me know if any issues arise in the meantime.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: focal motor seizures occur 3 - 4 per day

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter provides multiple seizure types with frequencies: 'generalised tonic-clonic seizures twice monthly' (i.e., 2 per month), 'focal motor seizures occur 3 - 4 per day', and 'drop attacks clustering every month.' Rule: choose the highest frequency among multiple types. Comparing 3 to 4 per day vs 2 per month vs monthly clusters, the highest frequency is 3 to 4 per day. There's a note 'No seizures have been noted since her previous review,' but this is a short-term interval and does not meet the 6-month seizure-free criterion, so we use the stated ongoing frequencies. Therefore, the normalized label is '3 to 4 per day.'
seizure_frequency_number: focal motor seizures occur 3 - 4 per day
reference: Over the last year, seizure control has been relatively stable; she suffers generalised tonic-clonic seizures twice monthly, with a maximum seizure-free span of five weeks. focal motor seizures occur 3 - 4 per day. She also has drop attacks clustering every month. No seizures have been noted since her previous review
